rulings.Case Title: RICHARD A DENTON VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Re Motion To Strike Defendants Hale And Mitchell From The Feha Claim In Plaintiffs Seventh Cause Of Action
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 6, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF RICHARD DENTON's Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Re Motion To Strike Defendants Hale And Mitchell From The Feha Claim In Plaintiffs Seventh Cause Of Action Plaintiff Richard Denton's motion for reconsideration of the court's oral ruling striking defendant Barbara Hale from the seventh cause of action for unlawful harassment in his second amended complaint is denied. The court will address the merits of this motion based on the September 25, 2018 hearing since no written order has yet been filed as to the ruling sought to be reconsidered. The EEOC documents are not new facts that warrant reconsideration. The court already had those documents when it made its prior ruling. Most significantly, Cole v. Antelope Valley Union High School Dist. (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1505, 1515 is on point authority that Ms. Hale needed to be named in the caption or body of the administrative charge for Mr. Denton to pursue her in this action. Nor is Mr. Denton's filing of an additional administrative charge after the September 25 hearing a new fact or circumstance that warrants reconsideration. However, the oral ruling giving Mr. Denton leave to amend remains. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: PETER MCDONALD ET AL VS. 830 LAKE STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 6, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANTs 830 LAKE STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION, MATTHIAS SPANLE, MARK SHER, and PAMELA YOON's Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities Defendants' motion to enforce settlement agreement is denied. Per the addendum to the settlement agreement, the "HOA through the ... property manager shall retain a surveyor to survey the parking spaces in the west garage." Defendants have not presented any evidence that a survey was done. While Mr. Spanle stated in his declaration that the "surveyor marked the lines precisely as marked on the recorded map," there is no evidence that such marking constituted the "survey" required by the settlement agreement. Moreover, no evidence was provided by the surveyor of what it did or could have done to comply with the terms of the settlement agreement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiffs are required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: JOHN N. IANNUCCILLO ET AL VS. O'NEILL CONSTRUCTION, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of And Motion For Summary Judgment Of Treanor Tiling
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 6, 2018, Line 9, CROSS DEFENDANT TREANOR TILING MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Treanor Tiling Defendant Treanor Tiling's motion for summary judgment is denied. Treanor's motion fails at the outset because it is premised on the assumption that the claims alleged in the second amended complaint are not implied contractual indemnity claims, yet liberally construing those claims, they are implied contractual indemnity claims. The economic loss doctrine does not apply to contractual claims. The joint obligation for an implied contractual indemnity claim can be based on the contractual obligation that a subcontractor owes to an owner. (See, e.g. Loduca v. Polyzos (2007) 153 Cal.App.4th 1334 (property owner is third party beneficiary of subcontract).) Accordingly, even without consideration of the disputed testimony of plaintiffs' expert, the motion must be denied due to Treanor's failure to satisfy its summary judgment motion burden. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Iannuccillos is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL P GUTA VS. CASEY SECURITIES LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiff And Cross-Defendant Michael P. Gutas Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 6, 2018, Line 3, Plaintiff And Cross-Defendant Michael P. Gutas' Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Plaintiff and cross-defendant Michael Guta's motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication on his complaint for breach of contract and common counts and Casey Securities, LLC's first amended cross-complaint for rescission, breach of contract, and fraud are denied in their entirety. There are triable disputes regarding the amount of money owed to Mr. Guta and whether the parties had a good faith disagreement about that sum. (Guta Dec., par. 12; Gasparini Dec., par. 4.) The court overrules Mr. Guta's objection to the Mr. Gasparini's declaration. The recital in the employment contract is not dispositive and the recital was merely an approximation. There is also a triable dispute whether Mr. Guta agreed to release the disputed debt in exchange for the employment contract and whether CSL reasonably relied on Mr. Guta's representations. (Gasparini Dec., pars. 8-11.) Wihelm v. Pray, Price, Williams & Russell (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1323, 1332 is distinguishable given the client-counsel relationship between CSL and Mr. Guta. Mr. Guta fails to maintain his summary motion burden as to CSL's breach of contract cause of action. Mr. Guta argues that he communicated the only written settlement offer that he received in the Meridian litigation and CSL is speculating about the uncommunicated offer. Mr. Gasparini's deposition does not show that CSL is unable to establish damages within the meaning of CCP 437c and Mr. Guta admitted that he failed to convey the offer. (Gasparini Depo., 191:23-192:1.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CSL is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: ALEXIS MASSOL VS. MINH GIAO KHAC LE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Minh Le, Terry Tran And Xuan Nhut Tran'S Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, December 6, 2018, Line 5, Defendants Minh Le, Terry Tran And Xuan Nhut Tran'S Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint Defendants Minh Giao Khac Le, Terry Tran, and Xuan Nhut Tran's demurrer to Plaintiff Alexis Massol's second amended complaint is sustained as to all ten causes of action without leave to amend. The third cause of action for violation of the Ellis Act and the fifth cause of action for violation of section 37.9A of the San Francisco Rent Ordinance are both time-barred. Government Code section 7060.2 and Rent Ordinance section 37.9A(d) establish a limitations period for bringing a claim regarding failure to offer to re-rent the premises of three years from the date of withdrawal of the unit. While Mr. Massol requested a one-year extension of the withdrawal, that request was disputed by the former landlord and Mr. Massol ultimately vacated the property on March 16, 2014 before the official withdrawal date. The judicially noticeable Notice of Constraint establishes that the San Francisco Rent Board determined the withdrawal date to be April 15, 2014. The allegation of a later withdrawal date in the second amended complaint is not accepted as true on this demurrer. Measured from April 15, 2014, the limitations period expired before Mr. Massol filed this action on August 15, 2017. Although the discovery rule tolling the limitations period applies to the second, fourth, and sixth causes of action for wrongful eviction and violations of Rent Ordinance sections 37.11A and 37.9(f), these claims fail because the new landlord-defendants did not unlawfully "recover" possession from Mr. Massol. Rather, they lawfully purchased the property from the previous owners after Mr. Massol had been evicted. While subsequent owners of rented property can be held liable under the definition of "landlord," the cases cited in the opposition are distinguishable because none of them involved subsequent property owners who took control of the property after the tenant was evicted. The failure to re-rent the unit would properly be alleged against subsequent owners under the Ellis Act and section 37.9A of the Rent Ordinance if those claims were not time-barred. The first cause of action for negligence fails because defendants owe no duty to Mr. Massol as they were never Mr. Massol's landlord and, as explained above, there is no basis for a statutory duty under the Ellis Act or Rent Ordinance. Similarly, the seventh through tenth causes of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress, unfair business practices, fraud, and declaratory and injunctive relief fail because those claims stem directly from the wrongful eviction and failure to re-rent claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk
